front: What is a Transaction in DBMS?
back: A transaction is a logical unit of work consisting of one or more database operations that are executed as a single logical unit of work. It represents a change in the database state.

front: What does ACID stand for in transaction properties?
back: ACID stands for Atomicity (all-or-nothing execution), Consistency (valid state transformation), Isolation (concurrent transactions appear serial), and Durability (committed changes persist).

Atomicity ensures that a transaction is treated as a single atomic unit - either all its operations are executed successfully, or none are executed. If any operation fails, the entire transaction is rolled back.
Consistency ensures that a transaction transforms the database from one valid state to another valid state, maintaining all database constraints and rules.
Isolation ensures that concurrent transactions appear to execute serially - the intermediate results of one transaction are not visible to other concurrent transactions until committed.
Durability ensures that once a transaction is committed, its effects are permanent in the database even if system failure occurs. Committed data is stored in non-volatile memory.
The five transaction states are: Active (executing), Partially Committed (last statement executed), Committed (successfully completed), Failed (cannot proceed), and Aborted (rolled back).

front: What is a Schedule in DBMS?
back: A schedule is a sequence of instructions specifying the chronological order in which instructions of concurrent transactions are executed. It represents the execution order of transactions.
A serial schedule is one where transactions are executed one after another without interleaving their operations. No two transactions execute concurrently.
Serializability is the property of a concurrent schedule producing the same result as some serial schedule of the same transactions. It ensures correctness while allowing concurrency.

Conflict serializability exists when a schedule can be transformed into a serial schedule by swapping non-conflicting operations. Conflicting operations are read-write, write-read, or write-write on same data item.
View serializability exists when a schedule produces the same result as some serial schedule in terms of read operations. It considers the initial read and final write of each data item.

front: What are conflicting operations in serializability?
back: Two operations conflict if they belong to different transactions, operate on the same data item, and at least one is a write operation. Types: read-write, write-read, and write-write conflicts.
A precedence graph (or serializability graph) is a directed graph used to test conflict serializability. Nodes represent transactions, and edges indicate conflicting operations.
Recoverability refers to the property that if a transaction fails, it should not affect committed transactions. A recoverable schedule ensures that committed transactions are never rolled back.
